user_query:
I’m trying to build a small, trustworthy reference library I can lean on when I feel overwhelmed by the baggage of growing up in the spotlight. Please curate and index evidence‑based resources tailored to former child actors like me, covering coping with fame, career transitions, peer support, and financial wellness.

Use the topic list in input/topics.yaml and filter out low‑quality sources using input/exclusions.txt. Here’s what I need:

1) Find and collect resources
- For each topic in input/topics.yaml, use a search engine to run queries based on the provided keywords.
- Select resources that meet all of the following criteria:
  • From official or authoritative sources: .org, .edu, or .gov domains; or established unions/guilds and non‑profit mental health organizations. 
  • The page is informational (not a paywall, ad page, or a single event listing), and is directly relevant to the topic.
  • Exclude any result whose URL or visible page text contains a term from input/exclusions.txt (case‑insensitive match).
- Coverage target: at least 2 unique resources per topic and at least 8 resources total (no duplicate URLs across the whole set).

2) Save raw pages locally
- For every accepted resource, save the fetched HTML to: output/raw/<topic-slug>/<id>.html
  • <topic-slug> is the topic name lowercased with spaces replaced by hyphens.
  • <id> is the lowercase hex SHA‑1 of the resource URL. 
- Also write a simple log of the exact search queries you used to output/queries_used.txt (one line per query including the topic name).

3) Build a structured index
- Create output/resources.csv with a header and one row per resource using these columns in order:
  id,url,topic,title,source_domain,tld_type,publication_year,retrieved_at,summary
  • id: the lowercase hex SHA‑1 of url (must match the filename used for the raw HTML).
  • url: canonical URL of the resource.
  • topic: exactly one of the topic names from input/topics.yaml.
  • title: page title extracted from the page.
  • source_domain: e.g., "example.org".
  • tld_type: one of org|edu|gov|com|other based on the source_domain.
  • publication_year: best effort extraction from the page (empty if unknown).
  • retrieved_at: ISO‑8601 timestamp when you saved the page.
  • summary: a concise plain‑text abstract (around 80–200 words) extracted from the page content.
- Ensure that for every CSV row, the corresponding raw HTML exists at output/raw/<topic-slug>/<id>.html.

4) Compute basic stats for verification
- Generate output/stats.json containing at least these fields, computed from output/resources.csv:
  • total_resources (integer; must equal the number of data rows in the CSV)
  • by_topic (object mapping topic name -> count)
  • by_tld_type (object mapping tld_type -> count)
  • unique_domains_count (integer)
  • top_domains (array of up to 3 objects with fields domain and count, sorted by count desc)
  • summary_word_count_mean (number, rounded to one decimal)
  • missing_publication_year_count (integer)

5) Reproducibility and execution
- Provide a runnable script at scripts/collect_resources (you may use .py or .sh) that reads input/topics.yaml and input/exclusions.txt and regenerates the outputs under output/ exactly as specified. I will re‑run it to verify.
- Please run your script once and include the generated output artifacts in the workspace.

Notes:
- Do not hardcode specific sites; use a search engine and the domain rules above to find suitable resources.
- Keep the Internet step essential by actually fetching and saving the pages you index.
- If a topic yields more than the minimum, include the strongest resources that meet the criteria; still enforce deduplication by URL.

Deliverables and paths:
- Script: scripts/collect_resources (executable; .py or .sh)
- Logs and raw: output/queries_used.txt and output/raw/<topic-slug>/<id>.html files
- Index: output/resources.csv (with the exact columns and order listed)
- Stats: output/stats.json (with the fields listed)

Inputs to use:
- input/topics.yaml
- input/exclusions.txt All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
